finding: An individual difference in contingency learning (predictive association between stimulus and response) explains item-specific effects on a cognitive task more completely than attentional modulation.
Sampling strategies that are optimal for one inferential task create systematic biases for others, creating a fundamental tension between unbiased sample design and valid inference.
Parallel retrieval performance across condition types supports a dual-process account of recognition where recollection uses multiple independent retrieval routes.
Encoding strength via context information improves directed forgetting of strong items, but strength via item-specific processing does not.
Bilingual individuals do not exceed monolinguals in active inhibitory control but show stronger maintenance of goal representations, which can indirectly increase reactive inhibition.
Automatic activation of a learned relation (addition facts) depends on whether semantic relations in preceding stimuli align with the structure of that learned relation.
Implicit learning in repeated sequences involves both learning correct responses and learning the errors that were previously produced, with both following the same repetition-frequency gradient.
Two stimulus factors show additive effects in means but opposite interactive effects in response-time distributions, revealing distinct underlying processing architectures depending on task demands.
Semantic interference persists even when target responses are delayed, but frequency effects disappear, indicating the interference effect operates at a post-lexical processing stage.
Feature learning in multidimensional sequences is object-based or feature-based depending on the degree of covariation between feature dimensions during encoding.
Causal judgments weight the capacity of each cause to account for outcome occurrences over base contingency, predicting overshadowing failures when causes differ in their explanatory power.
Memory for temporal order and timing information share mechanisms; temporal patterns in input are reproduced in output across different recall objectives.
Attention directed to element pairs is sufficient for learning their relation regardless of spatial contiguity, reframing contiguity effects as a byproduct of attentional distribution.
Retrospective attention to a single item in working memory reduces interference from new incoming information, demonstrating that memory contents require focused attention to resist updating.
Learning and decision-making processes adapt differentially to task structure; Bayesian learning interferes with risk assessment while decision-making increases its validity.
Individuals who commit to socially disapproved behavior resent moral dissenters, but uninvolved observers prefer them; resentment is mediated by threatened self-image.
Weak correlations between implicit and explicit attitude measures reflect differences in task structure rather than independence of underlying cognition; structural alignment increases correlations.
Self-report reliability improves with age but different personality domains show divergent trajectories; some gain coherence while others gain differentiation.
Mortality salience triggers reliance on parental attachment for security, but the target of this reliance (parent vs. romantic partner) depends on attachment style.
General goal primes (action vs. inaction) increase or decrease behavioral output uniformly across diverse motor and cognitive domains; effects vanish once the goal state is achieved.
